quote: Right. So that's the one that I'm concerned about. It's definitely putting quite a bit of shade. Are you I mean, my average
stewart_read: Kevin begins to volunteer his bill data mid-sentence — 'my average' — and Meg talks over him to press the shade concern. This is the earliest evidence of reactive posture: Meg is following her own problem-framing track rather than listening for the customer's signal. The bill anchor, which the schema identifies as the emotional center of the pitch, was being handed to Meg for free and she interrupted it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg was establishing the shade constraint first to set up a tree-removal conversation. Rejected — there is no subsequent setup that uses this framing productively, and the schema does not support leading with disqualifiers before bill anchoring.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the moment at 01:06 where Kevin starts 'my average' and she talks over it. Ask her: what was Kevin about to give you? The bill anchor is the schema's emotional center — when a customer volunteers it, you stop and receive it. Drill the listen-first reflex before the shade conversation.

stewart_read: By 00:37 Meg has confirmed address and home type. She then moves directly to the shade concern at 00:49. The schema requires 6-of-8 verify items; Meg collects 2 (address/homeowner confirmed, home type). Title/co-owners, utility company, roof type/age, prior designs, interest_in_solar, and residence length are never collected. The call ends without a single additional verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg planned to complete verify after resolving the shade concern. Rejected — the call ends at 02:44 with no verify items added after 00:37, and the shade concern was used to exit rather than to set up further qualification.
coaching_implication: Walk Meg through the verify checklist against this transcript. She collected 2 of 8 items. Ask her: if you had known Kevin had prior solar designs and a 21-year payback quote in hand, would you have handled the shade conversation differently? Verify is not box-checking — it is the intelligence that shapes every subsequent move.

quote: well, the highest bill I've ever had was a $109. And my average bill, I'd say, over a course of twelve months is around 76 to $81.
stewart_read: Kevin volunteers precise bill data — $76–$81 average, $109 peak — unprompted. This is the bill anchor the schema says must be captured AND flipped. Meg's response at 02:02 acknowledges the low bill ('your bill isn't extremely high') but does not flip it. She uses it as a secondary DQ rationale rather than as an emotional anchor. The flip was available: the bill Kevin is paying is the bill he will keep paying forever if solar does not work — the question is whether the shade analysis confirms that.
coaching_implication: Meg captured the bill data but dropped it immediately. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern (coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring) requires the bill to become emotional alignment against the utility, not a data point that supports the DQ. Ask Meg: what does Kevin's $81/month mean over 20 years? That number is the leverage — she had it and walked away from it.

quote: Breakeven over twenty one years. It used to be a thirty six year payback, so I don't know.
stewart_read: Kevin has done prior solar research and is citing specific payback figures (21-year breakeven, previously 36-year). This is a customer who has engaged with solar economics before — he is not uninformed, he is unconvinced. Meg does not address the payback math at any point. The schema calls for 3+ angles per objection; Meg deploys zero. The knowledge gap here is not Kevin's — it is Meg's failure to route a math-literate objection to the closer's design tools rather than accepting it as a DQ.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg recognized the payback objection was closer-tier content and chose not to engage it at setter scope. Rejected — the correct strategic move would have been to use the payback objection as the curiosity hook ('that's exactly what our specialist's design will show you — whether the numbers work for your specific home'). Instead Meg pivoted to shade as the exit, not as a bridge.
coaching_implication: Kevin's payback math is a closer-tier objection, not a setter-tier DQ. The setter's move is the universal escape hatch from coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — they'll run the actual shading analysis and show you whether the numbers work for your home.' Drill this redirect with Meg so she stops accepting math objections as DQs.

quote: I mean, regardless of their incentives or not, it's just I don't think it can be done. But, I mean, I'm more willing to listen.
stewart_read: Kevin explicitly signals residual openness — 'I'm more willing to listen' — after delivering his skepticism. This is a conditionally open customer, not a closed one. The signal is easy to miss because it is buried in a skeptical sentence, but it is the clearest buying signal in the call. Meg's next turn at 02:02 does not acknowledge it and instead confirms the DQ. The crack was real and the rep walked past it.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Kevin's skepticism ('I don't think it can be done') outweighs the residual openness and Meg correctly read him as a low-probability lead. Rejected — 'I'm more willing to listen' is a direct invitation that the schema's curiosity_over_features principle is designed to enter. A single curiosity hook here ('let me ask you this — have you ever seen a shading analysis run on your specific roof?') costs nothing and preserves the lead.
coaching_implication: Play the 01:44 clip for Meg and stop the recording. Ask her: what did Kevin just say? 'I'm more willing to listen' is the customer handing you the call back. The coaching move is to drill the curiosity hook that enters this crack — not a pitch, not a rebuttal, just a question that makes Kevin want to see his specific numbers.

quote: I think where where the situation is, where I'm at, right, is your home doesn't get enough sun exposure for a system to even work effectively.
stewart_read: Meg issues a shading DQ based on a Google Maps impression and Kevin's confirmed back-tree. The error is not that she had no shade data — Kevin confirmed the back tree at 01:00, so the shade concern was customer-validated. The error is concluding that confirmed shade is fatal rather than routing it to the closer's shading analysis software. Shade assessment is closer-tier content; the setter's job is to surface the concern and bridge, not to adjudicate it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg correctly identified a hard DQ condition (insufficient sun exposure) and saved the closer's time. Rejected — shade from a single back tree is not a schema hard DQ. The schema's hard DQ list (dq_rules) does not include 'partial shade.' The closer's shading tools exist precisely to evaluate cases like this.
coaching_implication: The coaching point is not 'don't trust your shade data' — Kevin confirmed the tree. The coaching point is: confirmed shade is a question for the closer's analysis tools, not a setter-level DQ. Meg needs to understand where her authority ends. Suggested redirect: 'Our specialist actually runs a shading analysis on your specific roof — that's what tells us whether the tree is a problem or not. Let me get you on the calendar so they can show you the actual numbers.'

quote: If things change, you know, if you ever wanna look at options, feel free to give me a call. I'm happy to help.
stewart_read: Meg's exit offer is a vague callback with no locked time, no curiosity hook, and no re-engagement structure. The schema distinguishes sharply between callbacks with locked times and 'we'll touch base' exits — the latter have materially higher no-show and no-callback rates. On a lead that still had residual openness at 01:48, this exit forfeits any chance of recovery.
coaching_implication: Even if Meg believed this lead was low-probability, the exit should have included a specific re-engagement hook: 'Kevin, you mentioned you've been watching the payback numbers improve — from 36 years down to 21. If you ever want to see what the actual shading analysis shows for your roof, I can get that set up in 30 minutes. Want me to put a placeholder on the calendar?' A locked time with a curiosity hook is recoverable. 'Give me a call if things change' is not.

quote: There are no trees in the front anymore. Just one in the back near the house.
stewart_read: Kevin's disclosure that the front trees are already gone is a meaningful sun-exposure signal — the primary shading concern Meg raised (Google Maps view) has already been partially resolved by Kevin himself. The remaining concern is a single back tree. Meg does not register the front-tree removal as a positive signal; she focuses only on the remaining back tree. A rep reading for opportunity would have anchored on the improvement: 'So the front is already clear — that's actually the best exposure for solar. Let me have our specialist look at what the back tree does to the design.'
coaching_implication: Kevin told Meg the front trees are gone — that is a sun-exposure improvement he has already made. The coaching move is to anchor on what is working before addressing what is not. Ask Meg: when a customer tells you the obstacle is smaller than you thought, what do you do with that? The answer is not 'but the back tree is still a problem.' The answer is 'great — let's find out how much of a problem the back tree actually is.'
